name: Gashadokuro
description: The Gashadokuro is a gigantic skeleton creature in Japanese mythology, fifteen times larger than an average human. These monstrous beings are formed from the amassed bones of people who died of starvation or in battle, unburied and filled with resentment. They roam the countryside after midnight, seeking to satiate their endless hunger by devouring living humans. The rattling of their bones, a deafening 'gasha gasha' sound, precedes their appearance, warning potential victims of their impending doom. A Gashadokuro can only be dispersed or appeased through Shinto rituals or by satisfying its hunger, although even then, its existence is tied to the collective sorrow and anger of the unburied dead. Their immense size and the sheer horror of their origin make them terrifying figures in Japanese folklore, embodying the consequences of neglect and the lingering pain of untimely death. They represent the restless spirits of the forgotten and the devastating power of unresolved trauma.
subtitle: Giant Skeleton of Unburied Dead
appearance: A Gashadokuro is a colossal skeleton, typically depicted as being at least fifteen times the size of a normal human. Its bones are often described as being stained with blood and grime, reflecting its gruesome origins. The eye sockets are empty, but a palpable sense of malice emanates from them. The bones perpetually rattle and grind against each other, creating a cacophonous sound that heralds its arrival. Its overall appearance is one of terrifying grandeur, a skeletal giant born from the collective trauma of countless deaths.
habitat: Gashadokuro typically haunt battlefields, mass grave sites, and other locations where large numbers of people have died violently or without proper burial rites. They are most active in rural areas, particularly at night, when the darkness conceals their massive forms. They are drawn to places where the echoes of suffering and resentment linger.
behavior: Gashadokuro are driven by an insatiable hunger for human flesh. They stalk their prey silently, using their immense size and surprising speed to ambush unsuspecting victims. They are said to be able to become invisible to those who are not destined to be their prey. Their primary motivation is to alleviate the pain and suffering that birthed them, though their methods are terrifying and destructive. The rattling of their bones can induce paralysis in weaker-willed individuals, making them easy targets.

keyMyths.title: The Battle of the Unburied
keyMyths.description: A Gashadokuro arose after a particularly brutal battle, formed from the unburied corpses left to rot on the field. Villagers who ventured near the site disappeared, their bones added to the giant's form.
keyMyths.source: Folk tales of the Sengoku period

keyMyths.title: The Starving Village
keyMyths.description: A famine-stricken village suffered the wrath of a Gashadokuro, born from the mass starvation of its inhabitants. The creature preyed on the few survivors, its hunger mirroring their own.
keyMyths.source: Local legends of the Edo period

sources.author: Shigeru Mizuki
sources.description: Mizuki's manga and anime series prominently features yokai, including the Gashadokuro, popularizing them in modern culture.
sources.text: Mizuki's manga and anime series prominently features yokai, including the Gashadokuro, popularizing them in modern culture.

sources.author: Unknown
sources.description: The Gashadokuro originates from various regional folk tales, passed down through generations.
sources.text: The Gashadokuro originates from various regional folk tales, passed down through generations.
